subject: Statistics
title: Incidence vs. Prevalence
description: A micro-lesson that makes Incidence vs. Prevalence usable.

hook.text: 10,000 people have the flu right now. Is that a lot? It depends: is the population 50,000 or 50 million?

buildup.text: Prevalence is the proportion currently affected. Incidence is the rate of new cases over time.

discovery.text: A chronic disease has high prevalence but low incidence — few new cases, but patients live with it for years.

twist.text: A deadly disease can have low prevalence despite high incidence. People get it often but die quickly.

climax.text: Confusing the two leads to wrong conclusions about whether a disease is spreading or just persisting.

punchline.text: How many have it now vs. how many get it new. Different questions.

quiz.question: What is the difference between incidence and prevalence?
options: Incidence counts new cases over time; prevalence counts all current cases at a point in time
Incidence is always higher than prevalence
They measure the same thing in different units
